comments.author: @henningvisser1108
comments.text:
(South African). During my working years, I had the privilege to visit Finland on 4 occasions, once in February (mid winter, -35 C) and once in June (mid summer, +30 C) and twice in between, all work related. I used to work for a Finnish mining tech company.
The Finns are an amazing people, but you first have to break through a façade before you can become friends.
The highlight of my visits was an excursion to Lapland. 
They brew a beer called Lapin Kulta (Lapland Gold) which is similar to our most popular brand.
I would love to go back but my time is running out.

comments.author: @aztecdune
comments.text: I moved to Finland from the US. I've found peace and tranquility here that I never had before. Yes, learning the language is a challenge, but it's worth it to be able to communicate with my Finnish friends and family. Happiness here is simple. When it's no longer dark on my bus ride home from work/school in January, I'm happy. When I sauna, I'm happy. When I eat my riisipiirakka and munavoi with my Finnish husband, I'm happy. Life doesn't need to be exciting. It just needs to be fulfilling.

comments.author: @Thamaell-San
comments.text:
One matter that the Finnish commenters didn't point out and seem to never point out when someone claims it, is that this stereotype - the image of the stoic, silent Finn who suffers in isolation and refuses to talk about their feelings - *is highly outdated.* The assumption that "Finns are averse to psychotherapy or seeking help for mental health issues," not a single statistics back this notion, which is a deep seated mis-attribution from the past, where there absolutely was resistance to seeking help for mental health issues - literally in every country on earth. This is still seen to this day most strongly in the eldest generations, but less so in their offspring, especially in the Nordics and some of the Anglo-Saxon countries. *If the reality of the matter interests you, keep on reading;*

*Finland is one of the least resistant countries to seeking help for mental health problems.* The problem is not cultural - the decades of active campaigning has increased the willingness to seek help for mental health services and the existing capacity and funding has been strained as a result. This is what the video did get right, as Finland needs and is building more capacity to it's mental health services. Similarly, as other commenters have pointed out, *Finland is near the EU average in Suicide statistics*, another misconception from the past that some foreigners in the comment sections seem to never update them self's on.

On Global scale, *Finland has been on the 35th to 40th highest rates for years now*- While the suicide rates have been declining in many countries, Finland's trajectory has been and still is a declining one. Currently Finland sits at 38th place globally and for comparisons sake the USA is at 31st place, having higher per capita rates than Finland. Worlds highest suicide rates are currently all in the more sunny regions of the world, top 10 having 6 African, 2 American and 2 Oceanian countries.

*Contrary to the popular myth that Finns succumb to the dark, freezing winter* (kaamos), data stretching back for over two centuries proves that *suicides in Finland consistently peak in May.* Endocrinologists and sociologists attribute this to rapid shifts in melatonin and serotonin caused by the sudden explosive return of sunlight, which can paradoxically spike severe agitation in deeply depressed individuals before their mood lifts.

🟥 1. HIGHEST CULTURAL RESISTANCE (Eastern Europe, Baltics & Balkans)
• *The Countries:* Ukraine, Belarus, Russia, Moldova, Romania, Bulgaria, Slovakia, Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia, Serbia, Bosnia & Herzegovina, Montenegro, Kosovo, Albania, North Macedonia, Georgia, Armenia, Azerbaijan.
• *The Cultural Barrier:* Deep-rooted institutional distrust. In many post-Soviet and former Eastern Bloc states, psychiatry historically carried a heavy association with political weaponization and punitive confinement. Therapy is often seen as a drastic last resort rather than a tool for personal growth.
• *The Reality:* Stigma index scores here are the highest in Europe. Mental health struggles are frequently viewed as personal or moral failures, leading to high self-stigma.

*Nuance Note:* Estonia is rapidly breaking away from this bracket due to intense digital and Nordic alignment, but traditional stigma remains stubborn among older generations.

🟧 2. MODERATE-TO-HIGH RESISTANCE (Southern Europe & Traditional Central Europe)
• *The Countries:* Greece, Poland, Hungary, Czechia, Italy, Portugal, Croatia, Cyprus, Malta, San Marino, Andorra, Monaco, Vatican City.
• *The Cultural Barrier:* Heavy reliance on tight-knit family structures and religious networks (Catholic or Orthodox). There is a powerful cultural expectation to resolve emotional or psychological distress "internally" through family support or pastoral counselling rather than involving outsiders.
• *The Reality:* While rapidly modernizing among younger demographics, seeking formal therapy still carries social friction. People are highly protective of their family’s public reputation, making them hesitant to seek formal psychiatric care.

🟨 3. MODERATE-TO-LOW RESISTANCE (Western & Central Europe)
• *The Countries:* France, Germany, Austria, Belgium, Spain, Switzerland, Luxembourg, Liechtenstein, Slovenia.

• *The Cultural Barrier:* High mental health literacy, but a strong cultural emphasis on *absolute privacy and professional self-reliance*.
• *The Reality:* In the DACH region (Germany/Austria/Switzerland), the default mindset leans toward "dealing with it on my own." While therapy is intellectually respected, individuals are highly guarded about disclosing it at work due to subtle career concerns. General Practitioners (GPs) are heavily relied on as defensive gatekeepers.

*Nuance Note:* Spain has made a massive cultural leap over the last decade, becoming one of the most openly pro-therapy nations in this bracket despite its Southern European geography. Slovenia also scores much closer to Western Europe than its Balkan neighbors due to robust healthcare infrastructure.

🟩 4. LOWEST CULTURAL RESISTANCE / HIGHEST OPENNESS (Nordic & Anglo-Saxon Europe)
• *The Countries:* Finland, Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Iceland, United Kingdom, Ireland, Netherlands.
• *The Cultural Barrier:* Virtually none. These are highly secular societies with decades of aggressive public awareness campaigns and heavily integrated, state-funded mental health care.
• *The Reality:* Seeking therapy has been stripped of its taboo status for the most part. It is widely viewed through the exact same lens as going to a physical therapist for a sports injury. Open conversations about mental well-being are completely normalized in schools, casual conversations, and corporate workplaces.

*The Systemic Takeaway:* The cultural resistance is for a large part tied directly to economics and infrastructure. Where the state covers psychological care seamlessly (e.g., the Netherlands, Finland, Norway), cultural resistance drops. Where care is entirely out-of-pocket or stuck behind years of waiting lists, the local culture adapts by labelling therapy as "unnecessary" as a psychological defence mechanism.

comments.author: @Thamaell-San
comments.text:
There is plenty of irony in the Finnish comments about how they don't like how Finland is romanticized in many videos. I agree with them, but let me explain why the irony;
When talking about the cultural ideals of any culture, it will easily sound romanticized and never fully respond to the reality. How ever, Finns who don't like that, come out to tell about it so straight, that it is just like the video claimed - un-boisterous honesty. I actually love it how this proves the point about some of the good sides of Finnish culture, even though that doesn't mean that every Finn is like that 😂
But yes, every culture has plenty of people, who don't correspond the cultural ideal of their country - that is just normal. But there is always an underlying reason, why these are called national cultural ideals, rather than "the whole truth".

a comprehensive evaluation of Finnish cultural traits demonstrates that Finland's modern social cohesion and egalitarian infrastructure are not just spontaneous developments of the 20th century. Instead, they represent the pragmatic institutionalization of ancient, subarctic survival strategies to a degree. The functional autonomy of the *emäntä* laid the foundation for early political franchising of women ; the consensus-seeking nature of the *käräjät* evolved into a highly trusted, decentralized public administration ; the collective labour of *talkoot* survived to maintain community infrastructure ; and the spatial logic of the *erämaa* was judicially codified to protect public access to nature. By preserving these traits through language, legal precedent, and cultural rituals, Finnish society maintains a distinct continuity with its historical origins, balancing ancient environmental pragmatism with the requirements of a modern, highly developed state.

In other words, Gender Equality, Social Trust & Consensus Governance, Communal Reciprocity (Talkoot), Spatial Access and Everyman's Right (Jokamiehenoikeus), The Sauna as a Sacred Liminal Sanctuary, Utilitarian Environmentalism and Linguistic Spatial-Cognitive Architecture (the evolution of the Finnish language reflecting the cultural evolution), can all find similarities in the ancient practises of the Finnic tribes, suggesting both linear and non-linear cultural links to modern times - why some aspects of modern Finnish culture is what it is today. Other Nordics have similar cultural traits than Finns, but we, just like every other Nordic nation, have our own peculiarities also.

1. Linguistic and Ethnic Roots
• *The Scandinavian Paradigm:* Swedish, Danish, and Norwegian are North Germanic languages derived from Old Norse and a common root language.
• *The Finnish Divergence:* Finnish is entirely unrelated to Germanic languages; it is a Uralic/Finno-Ugric language with origins in the boreal forest belt of the Ural Mountains, sharing structural and historical roots with Estonian and Hungarian. Consequently, Finns belong to a distinct Baltic-Finnic ethnic group with a separate socio-historical trajectory.

2. Communication Style: Consensus-Seeking vs. Direct Straightforwardness
• *The Scandinavian Paradigm:* Scandinavian communication, particularly in Sweden, is characterized by a strong emphasis on diplomatic phrasing, conflict avoidance (*konflikträdsla*), and extensive discussions designed to achieve absolute consensus.
• *The Finnish Divergence:* Despite sharing this consensus culture in politics, in communication habits Finns favour a highly direct, straightforward, and "no-nonsense" communication style, prioritizing brevity, efficiency, and literal honesty over social embellishments. Furthermore, silent pauses are meant to be treated as comfortable and natural moments of reflection rather than awkward gaps, and verbal agreements are considered legally and socially binding.

3. Leadership, Business, and Decision-Making
• *The Scandinavian Paradigm:* Scandinavian management is highly egalitarian, collaborative, and decentralized. Swedish leaders typically act as facilitators who gather input from all stakeholders before executing a decision, which can make the process feel slow or consensus-bound. Danish leadership is noted for being exceptionally informal and agile.
• *The Finnish Divergence:* While it is true that Finnish business and management culture is also mainly egalitarian, collaborative and non-hierarchical like in other Nordic nations, it is comparatively more formal, pragmatic, and action-oriented. Finnish leaders are comfortable at making rapid individual decisions and taking direct responsibility without requiring unanimous group consensus. For many Scandinavian partners, this directness can be perceived as blunt or commanding.

4. Core National Philosophies
• *The Scandinavian Paradigm:* Sweden’s national ethos is defined by *lagom* (the art of moderation and balance), Denmark's by *hygge* (coziness and homey warmth), and Norway’s by *kos* (shared intimacy).
• *The Finnish Divergence:* Despite agreeing with and sharing their own societal balance of the Scandinavian national philosophies (which is why many Nordic people consider the Nordic countries as a brotherhood, who are highly aligned culturally), Finland’s national philosophy is mainly centered around *sisu* - an extraordinary, quiet determination, mental toughness, and unyielding resilience to push through extreme adversity and harsh conditions, regardless of the odds.

5. The Ubiquity and Sacredness of the Sauna
• *The Scandinavian Paradigm:* Sweat bathing exists in Scandinavia (e.g., the Swedish *bastu*), but it is primarily treated as a recreational pastime or a private wellness option.
• *The Finnish Divergence:* The sauna in Finland is a national, historically sacred institution. With over 3.2 million saunas for a population of 5.5 million, it is an indispensable part of daily life, representing a democratic space of absolute physical equality where clothing and social hierarchies are shed.

6. Low Tolerance for Bragging:
While the "Law of Jante" (a Nordic cultural norm discouraging individual showiness) is present across all these nations, it is arguably applied with extra rigor in Finland.
Flaunting success is heavily discouraged, often leading to a modest and understated public persona, if not even to self depreciating social behaviour.
You can see this in practise in the way, that Swedes are often much better at marketing them self's, for example.

Because of having different cultural balances and strengths, a saying / joke was born that says;
"it would be a perfect business if the Danes managed it, Norwegians funded it, the Finns built it, and the Swedes marketed it."

comments.text: One can nitpick at the details, but the distinctions are fundamental.  I will compare my home country, the USA.  Finland emphasizes free and excellent education and health care for everyone.  This creates a sense of security.  Finns are less materialistic and more egalitarian.  The USA promotes social competition, which creates the potential for a greater reward, but this can be extremely stressfull, and most are left behind.  The USA also promotes the idea that everyone has an equal opportunity, but this is, frankly, laughable.

comments.author: @ronnystromberg
comments.text:
For me as a Finn, the two biggest reasons for happiness are

1. Money does not talk. Due to social security you do not have to fight for everything and try to impress, you can be yourself. The reason you want to perform is just a moral obligation and pride. That gives better results with less pressure.

2. 100% trust in government and fellow citizens, and law obediance. The mentality is built around the philosophy that the people around you always do their best to carry their straw within mutually agreed boundaries. 

Looking at those two, corruption is not even necessary. Pointless.

I am very happy, though life is miserable sometimes. I love my country, even if it has flaws.

comments.author: @etappi-7
comments.text:
A Finn here. Nice video but painting a rather romantisized picture of Finland and the Finnish life style. What also bothered me is some clear mistakes, like an American school bus   in one street plus numerous other clearly non-Finnish looking scenes with non-Finnish-looking people incl. the sauna scenes, a log truck passing through a city that for sure does not exist in Finland, rows of white bread depicting rye bread while claiming that white bread can never replace rye bread,  and then claiming that the drying cabinets in kitchens are for mittens and gloves. No they are not. They are for dishes.

Get your facts straight, and do not use AI to pretend that what you show is the real Finland.

replies.author: @tomituovinen3545
replies.text: ​@KuukelikyyGood that you enjoy there. A lot have changed from 1968 tough, good and bad...if you visit 2x per year you cannot nessecary realize what 😊 I know some Germans who live in Finland now...but I think it's more  which things one prefers and most important is that you enjoy. I just became officially retaired last year from govermental work ( työkyvyttömyyseläke ) I plan to move somewhere in Asia after my parents do not need me anymore ( pleasant way to say pass away ) Well I gladly take care of them. Anyway reason for me is that winter here makes my injuries more painful, but I still want to be here in summers. So reasons can be sometimes forcer, sometimes practical or just oppinions why someone wants to move to another country. No, I have wife and do not go there for women, but for culture, languages and well to escape winters 😅

replies.author: @Kuukelikyy
replies.text: A Finn here too; living in Hamburg, Germany since 1968. My social situation here is better than in Finnland. You don't replant an old busch (80 next year). Northern Germans don't talk much so I feel very familiar here!

replies.text: I agree. I'm not a Finn. But using internet and AI to b a tour guide is false PR. Share what u know personally.

replies.author: @tomituovinen3545
replies.text:
Finn also here and I agree 100%. First of all if you say something positive about Finland, Finns are first to depate otherwise. We are that kind of nation, I do not like that attitude too much, we could actually accept that we live in a good country and every country has their pos and cons.
But even more annoying are these paradise images. No country is paradise, if there was one we all would move there and it would stop being paradise.
There are 3 types of countries in the world. 1) countries that everybody, including their own country citizens think they are shit. Main reason to this the goverment. They have dictator, totalitarism and only few people can enjoy life. These countries can be rich or poor but main readon is corruption, war kinda state indide it and few people have abdolute powrr 2) Then there are ok or semi bad countries. Everything is kinda ok or little bit bad, but not mainly because of goverment but because location of country and lack of natural resources or education and country cannot develop. In these kinda countries people can be even very happy, but never rich. 3) Good countries. These counties are often lucky, they have natural resources or tech innovations and if goverment is ok or even good, they enjoy enough benefits that, that life in general is rather easy. This does not mean individuals are happy, becaude studies show that countries where everyone is doing extrimely well, people who do bad suffer even more because pressure doing well is huge. Thats why many good countries have still higher suicide rates. Hard to depressed among happy people.
There is no paradise, there are ups and downs everywhere. Only if you live country number 1 try to escape

replies.author: @cinderellaandstepsisters
replies.text:
Not true. Finland's suicide rates have gone drastically down in 30 years. We don't live in the 1990s anymore.  Watch the statistics in the video "Comparison suicide rates by countries/Country suicide rate comparison!"
There are 62 highest suicide countries and Finland is not even mentioned. 
The highest suicide countries are: The 1st RUSSIA. The 2nd South Korea. The 3rd Kazakstan. The 4th Ukraine and the 5 th Japan.

replies.author: @karvajalka72
replies.text: Just visit in June to August, +20C up to +30C (July) temperatures, couple of years we have had even +35C for 3-4 weeks in Southern Finland - I know these temperatures are nothing to some people living in much more warm climates, BUT they are not Cold Temperatures! Also in summer you can experience the phenomenon when sun does not go down at all (in North of Finland, but also in Helsinki only 2-4 hours the sun is below horizon but STILL giving some light to sky and not really the darkness at all)

comments.author: @ristovirtanen6396
comments.text:
The narrator needs to know something…🙄
Finnish pronunciation is simple: the stress is always on the first syllable and letters are pronounced the same independent of surrounding letters with some rare exceptions: ng or nk are pronounced the same as in English because there’s no letter representing that phoneme. And ä is pronounced like the vowel in sad or bad. Ö is pronounced like the vowel in girl, bird or first. And J is pronounced like Y in yes. One letter short, doubled long. 👍Can it get any simpler?🤔

replies.author: @gideonregalado5477
replies.text: You missed it. Finland has been through famines the worst of which was 1866-1868. Finns had to eat tree barks. The hilarious thing is that, from tree bark, they invented a cholesterol lowering margarine that you can buy from the grocery shops today (watch out, it is a little bit more expensive). And what about the milk from oats that you see in the coffee shops today? In 40 years that I have lived in Finland, I have seen how Finland has always rebounded (If Pura is tough, Viinanen was tougher). The economy once again promises a rebound (slowly but surely). This video has explained to you what is "sisu". One can lose his/her job but children still go to school for free. Nobody goes bancrupt when he/she gets sick. There is no need to set up "Go Fund Me" to be able to pay medical bills. Finland's failing economy is a multi-layered relative concept.

comments.author: @EpicEarthYTB
comments.text: Finland’s connection to nature is truly beautiful. This video captures the perfect balance between their modern economy and ancient traditions. I love the idea of a life measured by presence rather than accumulation.

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text: I completely agree—what stayed with me most wasn't the scenery, but the idea that success can be measured by peace, time, and connection to nature instead of just owning more. That's a perspective more of us could learn from.
